content: Has any sort of wrapper of struct file_operations been sent to the list yet?
AFAIK @Wedson Almeida Filho has not yet sent them to the list.
Heh, I think file_operations was probably the first abstraction I ever wrote, funny that it'll be one of hte later ones to land.
but there's an old(?) version sent by Ariel during puzzlefs patches: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20230609063118.24852-6-amiculas@cisco.com/

Boqun Feng said:

but there's an old(?) version sent by Ariel during puzzlefs patches: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20230609063118.24852-6-amiculas@cisco.com/

that one says it copied them from the rust branch though...

That's why I said it's old ;-) I assume Alice asked because of Christian's comment:

But bigger picture I somewhat struggle with the semantics of this
because this is not an interface that we have in C and this is really
about a low-level contract between C and Rust. Specifically this states
that this pointer is _somehow_ guaranteed valid. And really, this seems
a bit of a hack.

Naively, I think this should probably not be necessary if
file_operations are properly wrapped. Or it should at least be demotable
to a purely internal method that can't be called directly or something.

So what I mean is. fdget() may or may not take a reference. The C
interface internally knows whether a reference is owned or not by
abusing the lower two bits in a pointer to keep track of that. Naively,
I would expect the same information to be available to rust so that it's
clear to Rust wheter it's dealing with an explicitly referenced file or
an elided-reference file. Maybe that's not possible and I'm not
well-versed enough to see that yet.

Even old code may be helpful to demonstrate "what the wrapper of file_operations may look like".
Yeah, I wanted to reference it for my reply to that conversation. I wanted to know whether I should link to the old rust branch or whether we have it on the list somewhere.
Wedson also seems to have a newer version, due to his work on ext2: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/commit/276b977ff7f7f9858aac7fdb5084e8037911cd54
but it is split into multiple commits

Alice Ryhl said:

Yeah, I wanted to reference it for my reply to that conversation. I wanted to know whether I should link to the old rust branch or whether we have it on the list somewhere.

To me, File::from_ptr is at least needed for implementing file_operations abstraction itself.
Sure.
It seems that file_operations is in @Ariel Miculas 's rust-next-fs . this could be resolved if the branch merged?
I think Alice already sent the abstractions upstream: https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20240915-alice-file-v10-0-88484f7a3dcf@google.com/
@Alice Ryhl do you know when your file abstractions will be merged?
IIUC they have been merged: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240927-kicken-minigolf-de3ebd20f6ec@brauner/
so shouldn't they be in rust-next?
they are taken by the vfs tree
Christian brauner intends to send them upstream for 6.13 merge window
I need to familiarize myself with the kernel workflows
In general, the preference is that abstractions go through the tree for the subsystem that the abstractions wrap.
Ok, then they end up in linux-next, which rust-next is based off?
yes

Yeah, having everything in rust-next does not scale. One of the things we asked since the beginning was that maintainers should ideally take things through their tree. Exceptions may apply, of course.
They won't appear in rust-next at all

Alice Ryhl said:

They won't appear in rust-next at all

they will once Miguel rebases

Ariel Miculas said:

Ok, then they end up in linux-next, which rust-next is based off?

It depends on what you mean by based off. linux-next merges many branches, one of them is rust-next.
I mean that you rebase rust-next onto linux-next

Benno Lossin said:

they will once Miguel rebases

Since these are for v6.13, we will only see them in rust-next when I rebase for the next time, not this one, i.e. when I rebase this time around after Sunday, we will get everything in v6.12-rc1, but not things in linux-next meant for v6.13.
The linux-next branch is a merge of all of the -next branches. So it'll merge in rust-next and vfs-next and so on. Rust_next is not based on linux-next.

Ariel Miculas said:

I mean that you rebase rust-next onto linux-next

No, branches are not rebased on top of linux-next. It is the other way around -- linux-next merges branches like rust-next instead.
but the -rc tags are created in linux-next and those are used to rebase the other -next branches, right?
Ok, so my understanding was incorrect. Is there a doc that I should read on this topic?

Benno Lossin said:

but the -rc tags are created in linux-next and those are used to rebase the other -next branches, right?

or are they done in torvalds/master?
The rc tags are done from Linus' branch, which is different from linux-next
Linus's branch has stuff that's actually gone upstream. The linux-next branch is more comparable to rust-dev.

Benno Lossin said:

but the -rc tags are created in linux-next and those are used to rebase the other -next branches, right?

No, *-rc tags are created by Linus on mainline. linux-next creates the next-* tags instead. Branches like rust-next start typically in an -rc as you say, and then linux-next merges all those, and releases ~daily the next-* tags.

So, for instance, v6.12-rc1 will be the start point of rust-next in a few days, then I will for instance apply the lints series there, and then the next day linux-next will merge rust-next and have the lints series, and it will all be under a next-20241001 tag or similar.
linux-next is recreated from scratch every day, the usual branches like rust-next are not.
Then eventually I send a PR to Linus with what I have in rust-next. linux-next is not involved in that.
doesn't linux-next generate a lot of merge conflicts?

So it is true that eventually you will see things in rust-next that were in linux-next, but it is because it is the next cycle, where we start on top of a new -rc which contains all the previous cycle. It is also not completely true, because not everything in linux-next goes into mainline necessarily.

Benno Lossin said:

doesn't linux-next generate a lot of merge conflicts?

Part of the point of linux-next is, indeed, to figure out the conflicts and prepare for them.

I've read the patch @Alice Ryhl written. It seems that it also need more abstraction for file_operations. (right?) I hope that I can write a driver module for simple char device completely in rust. is it possible now?
I'm working on miscdevice: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240926-b4-miscdevice-v1-0-7349c2b2837a@google.com/
But no, char devices aren't possible right now. None of the drivers people have worked on have used that infrastructure.
You'll have to wait for miscdevice to land.
